1:
On 2 Corinthians 6:1 — “We then, as workers together with him, beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain” — the witnesses mark the appeal not to receive grace in vain.

The Eastern fathers note the urgency. Chrysostom: lest they grow supine, he “again alarms and arouses them.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “working together with him” (synergountes), partners with God (1 Corinthians 3:9); “in vain” (eis kenon), into emptiness. Wesley: received not in vain “if we add… persevering holiness.”

The verse follows the embassy with an earnest plea. As fellow-workers with God, we beseech you not to receive the grace of God in vain. The offered reconciliation must be truly embraced and not let slip.

So the witnesses see Paul press home the appeal of the embassy with a warning against wasted grace. The witnesses note the dignity and the partnership: the ministers are “workers together with him” — co-labourers with God in the work of grace, though wholly dependent on him. They mark the entreaty: having proclaimed reconciliation, Paul beseeches the Corinthians not to “receive the grace of God in vain” — not to let the offered salvation come to nothing, whether by failing to embrace it, or by professing it without the persevering holiness that proves it real. They observe Chrysostom’s point that Paul rouses them lest they grow careless, and Wesley’s that grace is received not in vain only when joined to continuing holiness. They note that even the work of Christ on the cross and the pleas of the ambassador may be nullified by the hearer who neglects so great salvation. The verse thus turns the doctrine of reconciliation into urgent exhortation: the grace held out must be received in earnest and held fast; to receive it idly, or to fall away, is to receive it in vain. It prepares the “now” of the accepted time.

So the witnesses see Paul press home the appeal of the embassy with a warning against wasted grace. The ministers are “workers together with him” — co-labourers with God, though wholly dependent on him. Having proclaimed reconciliation, Paul beseeches the Corinthians not to “receive the grace of God in vain” — not to let the offered salvation come to nothing, whether by failing to embrace it or by professing it without persevering holiness. Chrysostom notes Paul rouses them lest they grow careless. Even the work of Christ and the pleas of the ambassador may be nullified by the hearer who neglects so great salvation.
2:
On 2 Corinthians 6:2 — “(For he saith, I have heard thee in a time accepted, and in the day of salvation have I succoured thee: behold, now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation.)” — the witnesses mark the urgency of the present.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: a “Pauline parenthesis” quoting Isaiah 49:8; “acceptable time” (euprosdektos), well-received. Adam Clarke: the advent of the Messiah is the “accepted time.” The Reformed Calvin: the prophet speaks “of the kingdom of Christ.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: “the present is the season of confession… in an acceptable time.”

The verse seals the appeal with Scripture and the emphatic “now.” God says he heard and succoured in the accepted time; and behold, now is that accepted time, now the day of salvation. The hour of grace is the present.

So the witnesses see Paul press the urgency of the present moment of grace. The witnesses note the citation from Isaiah, originally of the Messiah and his kingdom, in which God promises to hear and help in the “accepted time” — the appointed season of favour. They mark how Paul applies it with a double “behold, now”: the prophesied day of salvation has dawned in the gospel; this very time, the age of grace and the present opportunity, is the accepted time. They observe the force against delay: since now is the day of salvation, the grace offered must be received now, not put off; the reconciliation is to be embraced while the season of mercy lasts. They note Cyril of Jerusalem’s pastoral application — now is the season of confession and of receiving the heavenly treasure. The verse thus intensifies the plea of verse 1: not only must grace not be received in vain, but it must be received now, in the present accepted time. The gospel hour is the present; the appeal “be reconciled” admits no postponement. This urgency frames the account of Paul’s faithful ministry that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul press the urgency of the present moment of grace. The citation from Isaiah, originally of the Messiah and his kingdom, has God promise to hear and help in the “accepted time.” Paul applies it with a double “behold, now”: the prophesied day of salvation has dawned in the gospel; this very time is the accepted time. The force is against delay: since now is the day of salvation, the grace offered must be received now, not put off. Cyril of Jerusalem adds the pastoral note — now is the season of confession and of receiving the heavenly treasure. The gospel hour is the present.
3:
On 2 Corinthians 6:3 — “Giving no offence in any thing, that the ministry be not blamed” — the witnesses mark the care to guard the ministry.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the aim. Robertson: “giving no occasion of stumbling” (proskopen) — that the ministry “be not blamed” (momethe). Barnes: no occasion for “contemning or rejecting the gospel.” Adam Clarke: any scandal whereby Jews or Gentiles might be stumbled. The Reformed Calvin: Paul commends both the ministry generally and his own integrity.

The verse states Paul’s scrupulous care. We give no offence in anything, that the ministry be not blamed. The credit of the gospel ministry must not be marred by the minister’s conduct.

So the witnesses see Paul introduce the account of his faithful conduct with a concern for the ministry’s honour. The witnesses note the aim: to give “no offence” — no cause of stumbling, no occasion by which any might despise or reject the gospel — so “that the ministry be not blamed.” They mark that the concern is not for Paul’s reputation merely but for the credit of the sacred office and the gospel it serves; a minister’s misconduct brings reproach on the message itself. They observe Calvin’s point that Paul commends both the dignity of the ministry in general and his own personal uprightness, the two intertwined. They note the diligence implied: in “any thing,” in every particular, he watches lest he furnish an occasion for the gospel to be blamed. The verse thus opens the great catalogue of his ministry that follows: having urged the Corinthians to receive grace and reconciliation, Paul shows how he and his fellow-labourers have so conducted themselves as to put no obstacle in the way, but rather to commend the ministry by patience, purity, and suffering — the credentials of a true servant of God.

So the witnesses see Paul introduce the account of his faithful conduct with a concern for the ministry’s honour. The aim is to give “no offence” — no cause of stumbling, no occasion by which any might despise or reject the gospel — so “that the ministry be not blamed.” The concern is not for Paul’s reputation merely but for the credit of the sacred office; a minister’s misconduct brings reproach on the message itself. Calvin notes Paul commends both the dignity of the ministry and his own uprightness. In “any thing,” in every particular, he watches lest he furnish an occasion for the gospel to be blamed.
4:
On 2 Corinthians 6:4 — “But in all things approving ourselves as the ministers of God, in much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses” — the witnesses mark the opening of the catalogue.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the structure. Robertson: a “marvellous summary… under three aspects” — first with “in” (en). Ellicott: “as ministers of God commending ourselves” — he looks back on a life of labour. The Reformed Calvin: the enumeration shows “all the tests… were to be found in Paul.” Wesley: “such as his ministers ought to be.”

The verse begins the great list of ministerial credentials. In everything we commend ourselves as God’s ministers: by much patience, in afflictions, necessities, distresses. The proof of a true minister is endurance under trial.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the famous catalogue by which he commends his ministry — not by outward credentials, but by faithful endurance. The witnesses note that he does “commend” himself, but in a way utterly unlike the false teachers: not by show or boasting, but by the tests of a true servant of God. They mark that “much patience” heads the list as the comprehensive grace, the steadfast endurance that bears all the rest. They observe the first triad of general trials — “afflictions, necessities, distresses” — the pressures, wants, and straits of his calling, broad terms for the whole weight of suffering he bore for the gospel. They note Calvin’s point that every test by which God tries his servants was found in Paul, so that the genuineness of his ministry was fully proved. The verse thus opens the long passage in which Paul vindicates his apostleship by his sufferings and graces: the true minister of God is approved not by appearance but by patient endurance of every hardship. It is the credential of the cross, the mark of fidelity, which the following verses detail.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the famous catalogue by which he commends his ministry — not by outward credentials but by faithful endurance. He does “commend” himself, but unlike the false teachers: not by show, but by the tests of a true servant of God. “Much patience” heads the list as the comprehensive grace that bears all the rest. The first triad of general trials — “afflictions, necessities, distresses” — names the pressures, wants, and straits of his calling. Calvin observes that every test by which God tries his servants was found in Paul, so that the genuineness of his ministry was fully proved. It is the credential of the cross.
5:
On 2 Corinthians 6:5 — “In stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labours, in watchings, in fastings” — the witnesses mark the particular sufferings.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify each. Robertson: “stripes” (plegais, blows, Acts 16:23); “tumults” (akatastasiais), instabilities from politics; “watchings” (agrypniais), sleeplessness. Ellicott: stripes at Philippi (Acts 16:23), and 2 Corinthians 11:23–24. Wesley: “tumults… such attacks as a man cannot stand against.”

The verse lists specific hardships. In stripes, imprisonments, tumults, labours, sleepless nights, and fastings. Paul names the concrete sufferings by which his ministry was tried and proved.

So the witnesses see Paul descend from the general to the specific, naming the concrete trials of his service. The witnesses note the first three as sufferings inflicted by others: “stripes” — the scourgings and beatings he endured, as at Philippi and in the synagogues; “imprisonments” — the bonds for the gospel; and “tumults” — the riots and mob-violence stirred up against him, attacks no man could withstand. They mark the next three as hardships freely borne in the work: “labours” — the toil of his ministry and his own hands; “watchings” — the sleepless nights spent in prayer, travel, or labour; and “fastings” — the hunger and want, whether voluntary or from poverty. They observe that the catalogue is no boast of merit but a record of fidelity, the marks of a true minister who spared not himself. The verse thus details the cost of Paul’s apostleship: a life of blows, bonds, riots, toil, sleeplessness, and hunger, endured for Christ and the gospel. These sufferings, borne in patience, are his credentials — the badge of a genuine servant of God, far weightier than the false teachers’ outward show.

So the witnesses see Paul descend from the general to the specific, naming the concrete trials of his service. The first three are inflicted by others: “stripes” — the scourgings he endured, as at Philippi; “imprisonments” — the bonds for the gospel; and “tumults” — the riots and mob-violence, attacks no man could withstand. The next three are hardships freely borne: “labours” — the toil of his ministry and his own hands; “watchings” — sleepless nights; and “fastings” — hunger and want. The catalogue is no boast of merit but a record of fidelity, the marks of a true minister who spared not himself.
6:
On 2 Corinthians 6:6 — “By pureness, by knowledge, by longsuffering, by kindness, by the Holy Ghost, by love unfeigned” — the witnesses mark the inward graces.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the turn to character. Robertson: “love unfeigned” (agape anypokrito), the only love worth while (Romans 12:9). Adam Clarke: “pureness… simplicity of intention… chastity and holiness.” Ellicott takes “pureness” as chastity. Wesley: “love unfeigned — the chief fruit of the Spirit.”

The verse turns from sufferings to graces. By purity, knowledge, longsuffering, kindness, by the Holy Spirit, by sincere love. The true minister commends himself not only by what he endures but by what he is.

So the witnesses see Paul pass from the outward trials to the inward graces that adorn the ministry. The witnesses note that the credentials of a true servant include not only sufferings endured but holy character displayed: “pureness” — purity of motive and of life, chastity and holiness; “knowledge” — understanding of the divine mysteries; “longsuffering” — patient forbearance under provocation; and “kindness” — gentleness even toward persecutors. They mark that these graces are not self-produced but flow “by the Holy Ghost” — the Spirit being both the source of the graces and himself a credential, working through the minister. They observe that the list culminates in “love unfeigned” — sincere, unhypocritical love, which Wesley calls the chief fruit of the Spirit and the crown of all. They note that this inward holiness answers the false teachers’ mere outward show. The verse thus completes the portrait of the true minister: approved not only by patient suffering but by a pure, knowing, forbearing, kind, Spirit-filled, and sincerely loving heart — the graces that commend the ministry far beyond any external credential.

So the witnesses see Paul pass from the outward trials to the inward graces that adorn the ministry. The credentials include not only sufferings endured but holy character: “pureness” — purity of motive and life; “knowledge” — of the divine mysteries; “longsuffering” — patient forbearance; and “kindness” — gentleness even toward persecutors. These graces are not self-produced but flow “by the Holy Ghost,” the Spirit being both their source and himself a credential. The list culminates in “love unfeigned” — sincere, unhypocritical love, which Wesley calls the chief fruit of the Spirit and the crown of all.
7:
On 2 Corinthians 6:7 — “By the word of truth, by the power of God, by the armour of righteousness on the right hand and on the left” — the witnesses mark the equipment of ministry.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “on the right hand and on the left” — offensive weapons on the right, defensive on the left (Ephesians 6:11); “weapons of righteousness.” Adam Clarke: “the word of truth… the power of God… confirming this doctrine.” Wesley: “the panoply or whole armour of God.”

The verse names the means of the minister’s work. By the word of truth, by the power of God, by the armour of righteousness on right and left. The true servant works by truth, divine power, and a complete panoply of righteousness.

So the witnesses see Paul name the instruments and equipment by which he carries on his ministry. The witnesses note “the word of truth” — the faithful, undiluted preaching of the gospel, the truth received from God and delivered without corruption. They mark “the power of God” — the divine energy accompanying the word, confirming it by miracles and, above all, by its converting and sanctifying effect on souls; the minister works not by human eloquence but by God’s power. They observe the military figure: “the armour of righteousness on the right hand and on the left” — a complete panoply, offensive weapons (the sword) in the right hand, defensive (the shield) on the left, all of “righteousness,” rectitude of conscience and holiness of life, the only armour that stands the strain. They note Calvin’s point that all God’s servants must fight, the devil ever on the alert. The verse thus completes the minister’s credentials with his equipment: truth, divine power, and the whole armour of righteousness — fully furnished for the warfare of the gospel, on every side.

So the witnesses see Paul name the instruments and equipment of his ministry. “The word of truth” is the faithful, undiluted preaching of the gospel. “The power of God” is the divine energy accompanying the word, confirming it by miracles and, above all, by its converting and sanctifying effect; the minister works not by human eloquence but by God’s power. The military figure — “the armour of righteousness on the right hand and on the left” — pictures a complete panoply, offensive weapons in the right hand, defensive on the left, all of righteousness. Calvin notes all God’s servants must fight, the devil ever on the alert.
8:
On 2 Corinthians 6:8 — “By honour and dishonour, by evil report and good report: as deceivers, and yet true” — the witnesses mark the paradoxes of ministry.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrasts. Robertson: “by glory and dishonour” (doxes kai atimias), state or condition now; “evil report and good report” (dysphemias kai euphemias). Adam Clarke: sometimes respected, sometimes despised; “as deceivers… and yet true.” Wesley: “as deceivers — so the world represents all true ministers… yet true.”

The verse begins a series of paradoxes. Through honour and dishonour, evil report and good report; counted deceivers, yet true. The minister passes through opposite estimates, but is what God knows him to be.

So the witnesses see Paul describe his ministry through a striking series of contrasts — the world’s shifting verdicts against the abiding reality. The witnesses note the first pairs: “honour and dishonour” — sometimes esteemed, sometimes despised; “evil report and good report” — now praised, now slandered, his name passed through both fair and foul speech. They mark that the true minister must hold his course through both, not lifted up by honour nor cast down by disgrace. They observe the first of the great antitheses: “as deceivers, and yet true” — the world brands the faithful preachers as impostors, frauds carrying about a false doctrine for gain, yet they are genuine, true to God and to their message. They note Wesley’s point that this is the world’s standing slander against all true ministers. The verse thus opens the famous catalogue of paradoxes that runs to verse 10: the servant of God lives amid the world’s contradictory judgments and false charges, yet remains, in God’s sight and in truth, what he is. The contrast between appearance and reality, the world’s scorn and the servant’s genuineness, is the theme — the minister approved not by men’s verdicts but by the truth of what he is in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul describe his ministry through a striking series of contrasts — the world’s shifting verdicts against the abiding reality. The first pairs: “honour and dishonour” — sometimes esteemed, sometimes despised; “evil report and good report” — now praised, now slandered. The true minister holds his course through both. The first great antithesis is “as deceivers, and yet true” — the world brands the faithful preachers as impostors, yet they are genuine, true to God and to their message. Wesley notes this is the world’s standing slander against all true ministers. The contrast between appearance and reality is the theme.
9:
On 2 Corinthians 6:9 — “As unknown, and yet well known; as dying, and, behold, we live; as chastened, and not killed” — the witnesses mark further paradoxes.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrasts. Robertson: “as unknown and yet well known” (agnooumenoi kai epiginoskomenoi) — ignored as nonentities, yet fully recognized by those who matter; “and behold, we live” (the hazards of his life). Adam Clarke: persons of no respectable connections, yet proved holy and useful. Wesley: “unknown — for the world knoweth us not… yet well known to God.”

The verse continues the paradoxes. As unknown, yet well known; as dying, yet we live; as chastened, yet not killed. The minister’s real state belies the world’s estimate and even his constant perils.

So the witnesses see Paul press on through the contrasts between the world’s view and the truth. The witnesses note “as unknown, and yet well known” — treated as obscure nobodies, men of no rank or credentials, ignored by the great; yet well known to God, and recognized by all who truly matter, the seals of his ministry. They mark “as dying, and, behold, we live” — constantly exposed to death, given up for dead, yet preserved alive by God’s power, the “behold” marking the wonder of repeated deliverance. They observe “as chastened, and not killed” — afflicted and disciplined, as if under God’s rod, yet not destroyed, echoing the psalm, “the Lord hath chastened me sore, but hath not given me over unto death.” They note the pattern: the outward appearance is of obscurity, death, and chastisement, but the reality is recognition, life, and preservation. The verse thus continues the great series: the servant of God seems, by the world’s reckoning and his own perils, to be nothing and perishing, yet in truth is known to God, kept alive, and spared — the paradox of the cross-shaped ministry, sustained by divine power.

So the witnesses see Paul press on through the contrasts between the world’s view and the truth. “As unknown, and yet well known” — treated as obscure nobodies, ignored by the great, yet known to God and recognized by those who truly matter. “As dying, and, behold, we live” — constantly exposed to death, given up for dead, yet preserved by God’s power, the “behold” marking the wonder of repeated deliverance. “As chastened, and not killed” — afflicted as under God’s rod, yet not destroyed. The appearance is of obscurity, death, and chastisement; the reality is recognition, life, and preservation.
10:
On 2 Corinthians 6:10 — “As sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, and yet possessing all things” — the witnesses mark the climax of the paradoxes.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrasts. Robertson: “always rejoicing” even in sorrow (Philippians 4:4); “making many rich” (ploutizontes), spiritual riches (1 Corinthians 1:5); “possessing all things.” Wesley: “having nothing, yet possessing all things — for all things are ours, if we are Christ’s.”

The verse crowns the paradoxes. As sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; as poor, yet enriching many; as having nothing, yet possessing all. The minister’s outward want hides an inward and bestowed wealth.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the series of contrasts to its glorious height. The witnesses note “as sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing” — outwardly a man of grief, weighed down by afflictions and by the sins and sufferings of others, yet inwardly always rejoicing in present peace and the hope of glory, a joy no sorrow could quench. They mark “as poor, yet making many rich” — himself destitute of earthly goods, yet enriching multitudes with the unsearchable riches of Christ, the spiritual wealth of the gospel. They observe the climactic “as having nothing, and yet possessing all things” — owning nothing the world counts wealth, yet possessing all in Christ; for, as Wesley says, all things are ours if we are Christ’s. They note the magnificence of the paradox: the poorest of men is the richest, the most sorrowful the most joyful. The verse thus completes the catalogue of the true minister’s estate: stripped by the world, yet abounding; the very emblem of the gospel’s reversal, where the servant of God, having nothing, possesses everything, and in his deepest poverty makes others eternally rich.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the series of contrasts to its glorious height. “As sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing” — outwardly a man of grief, yet inwardly always rejoicing in present peace and the hope of glory, a joy no sorrow could quench. “As poor, yet making many rich” — himself destitute, yet enriching multitudes with the unsearchable riches of Christ. The climactic “as having nothing, and yet possessing all things” — owning nothing the world counts wealth, yet possessing all in Christ; for, as Wesley says, all things are ours if we are Christ’s. The poorest of men is the richest — the gospel’s reversal.
11:
On 2 Corinthians 6:11 — “O ye Corinthians, our mouth is open unto you, our heart is enlarged” — the witnesses mark the affectionate appeal.

The Eastern fathers note the turn. Chrysostom: having detailed his trials and shown them a great good, he turns to direct entreaty. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “our mouth is open” (aneoigen, stands open) — he has kept back nothing; “our heart is enlarged” (peplatyntai). The Puritan Henry: a profession “of the most tender affection.” Wesley: “with uncommon freedom, because our heart is enlarged.”

The verse breaks into direct, tender address. O Corinthians, our mouth is open to you, our heart is enlarged. Paul speaks freely and fully because his heart is full of love for them.

So the witnesses see Paul pause from the catalogue to pour out his affection for the Corinthians. The witnesses note the rare direct address, “O ye Corinthians” — used seldom, here with peculiar tenderness, marking the height of his emotion. They mark the two figures: “our mouth is open unto you” — he has spoken with the utmost frankness and freedom, holding nothing back in his portrayal of the ministry’s glory and his own heart; and “our heart is enlarged” — expanded in love, wide enough to embrace them all and their interests. They observe that the open mouth flows from the enlarged heart: fullness of affection produces freedom of speech. They note Chrysostom’s observation that, having shown his sufferings and their blessedness, Paul now turns to affectionate entreaty. The verse thus marks the emotional climax of the passage: the apostle, having vindicated his ministry, lays bare his loving heart, speaking to the Corinthians as a father to beloved children. It prepares the gentle reproach of the next verses — that the straitness is not in his love but in theirs — and his plea for an answering affection.

So the witnesses see Paul pause from the catalogue to pour out his affection for the Corinthians. The rare direct address, “O ye Corinthians,” is used seldom, here with peculiar tenderness, marking the height of his emotion. Two figures: “our mouth is open unto you” — he has spoken with the utmost frankness, holding nothing back; and “our heart is enlarged” — expanded in love, wide enough to embrace them all. The open mouth flows from the enlarged heart: fullness of affection produces freedom of speech. The apostle, having vindicated his ministry, lays bare his loving heart, speaking as a father to beloved children.
12:
On 2 Corinthians 6:12 — “Ye are not straitened in us, but ye are straitened in your own bowels” — the witnesses mark the gentle reproach.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “ye are not straitened in us” (ou stenochoreisthe) — no restraint in Paul’s heart; the adversaries may have caused them to “tighten up” their affections (splanchna). Adam Clarke: the metaphor of one “pent up in a small… place.” Wesley: “our heart is wide enough to receive you all… your hearts are shut up.”

The verse locates the narrowness in them, not in him. You are not cramped in us, but in your own affections. Paul’s heart is wide; theirs has grown narrow toward him.

So the witnesses see Paul gently reproach the Corinthians for the coldness of their love. The witnesses note the figure continued from the enlarged heart: there is no “straitness” in Paul — his affection is roomy, with ample place for them and a thousand others; they are not cramped or shut out of his heart. They mark where the narrowness lies: “in your own bowels” — in their own affections, which had grown contracted and cold toward their apostle, perhaps under the influence of the false teachers who sought to alienate them. They observe the tenderness of the reproach: Paul does not accuse them harshly but points out, with a father’s sorrow, that the failure of fellowship is on their side, not his. They note Wesley’s plain summary — his heart is wide enough to receive them all, but their hearts are shut up. The verse thus turns the affectionate appeal into a loving complaint: the distance between them is of their own making, their love straitened while his is enlarged. It prepares the plea of the next verse — that they make a fair return, opening their hearts to him as he has opened his to them.

So the witnesses see Paul gently reproach the Corinthians for the coldness of their love. There is no “straitness” in Paul — his affection is roomy, with ample place for them and a thousand others; they are not cramped or shut out of his heart. The narrowness lies “in your own bowels” — in their own affections, grown contracted and cold toward their apostle, perhaps under the false teachers who sought to alienate them. Paul does not accuse them harshly but points out, with a father’s sorrow, that the failure of fellowship is on their side. His heart is wide enough to receive them all, but their hearts are shut up (Wesley).
13:
On 2 Corinthians 6:13 — “Now for a recompence in the same, (I speak as unto my children,) be ye also enlarged” — the witnesses mark the plea for an answering love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the appeal. Robertson: “recompense in like kind” (antimisthian), a rare word, paying back. Ellicott: children should requite the love of parents (1 Corinthians 4:15). The Reformed Calvin: he softens the reproof by calling them his sons. Wesley: “I ask nothing hard… open your hearts.”

The verse makes the loving request. By way of fair return — I speak as to my children — be enlarged also. Paul asks only that they open their hearts to him as he has to them.

So the witnesses see Paul press his plea for a mutual love. The witnesses note the appeal for reciprocity: “a recompence in the same” — a fair return in kind, that as he has opened his heart to them, they should open theirs to him. They mark the tenderness of the address, “I speak as unto my children” — he claims a father’s right, for he had begotten them in the gospel, and children owe their parents an answering affection. They observe that the request is modest and gentle: he asks nothing hard or grievous, no money or service, only their love — “be ye also enlarged,” widen your narrowed hearts to receive him. They note Calvin’s observation that calling them his sons softens the reproof and shows he still hopes well of them. The verse thus completes the affectionate appeal: the father asks his children for the one recompense he desires, their open-hearted love. It is the close of the tender interlude — and, having pleaded for their enlarged affection, Paul will at once turn to the holy condition of true fellowship: separation from the defiling alliances of unbelief.

So the witnesses see Paul press his plea for a mutual love. He appeals for reciprocity: “a recompence in the same” — a fair return in kind, that as he has opened his heart to them, they should open theirs to him. The tender address, “I speak as unto my children,” claims a father’s right, for he had begotten them in the gospel. The request is modest: he asks nothing hard, no money or service, only their love — “be ye also enlarged.” Calvin observes that calling them his sons softens the reproof and shows he still hopes well of them. The father asks his children for the one recompense he desires, their open-hearted love.
14:
On 2 Corinthians 6:14 — “Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? and what communion hath light with darkness?” — the witnesses mark the call to separation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “unequally yoked” (heterozygountes), from the law against yoking different beasts (Leviticus 19:19; Deuteronomy 22:10). Adam Clarke: a military term, “keep in your own ranks.” Wesley: “particularly… marriage… but… against any needless intimacy.” The Reformed Calvin: he reproves their associating in idolatry.

The verse commands separation from defiling alliances. Be not unequally yoked with unbelievers; for righteousness has no fellowship with unrighteousness, nor light with darkness. The believer must not bind himself to the ungodly.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the plea for love to the holy condition of true fellowship: separation from unequal alliances with unbelief. The witnesses note the figure drawn from the law: as Israel was forbidden to yoke an ox with an ass or to plough with diverse beasts, so the believer is not to be “unequally yoked” with unbelievers — bound in intimate, binding union with the ungodly. They mark that this applies especially to marriage, but also, as Wesley notes, to any needless intimacy or partnership that compromises faith, and, as Calvin stresses, particularly to sharing in idolatry. They observe the first two of five pointed questions that follow, each exposing the incompatibility: “what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness?” and “what communion hath light with darkness?” — opposites that cannot truly mix. They note that the call is not to abandon all contact with the world (which is impossible) but to refuse the binding alliances that join the holy to the unholy. The verse thus opens the appeal to holiness and separation: the people reconciled to God, the temple of the living God, must not yoke themselves to the unbelief and idolatry from which they have been delivered.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the plea for love to the holy condition of true fellowship: separation from unequal alliances with unbelief. The figure is drawn from the law: as Israel was forbidden to yoke diverse beasts, so the believer is not to be “unequally yoked” with unbelievers — bound in intimate union with the ungodly. This applies especially to marriage, but also to any needless intimacy that compromises faith, and particularly to sharing in idolatry (Calvin). The first two of five pointed questions expose the incompatibility: righteousness has no fellowship with unrighteousness, nor light with darkness. The call is not to abandon all contact but to refuse binding alliances.
15:
On 2 Corinthians 6:15 — “And what concord hath Christ with Belial? or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?” — the witnesses mark the third and fourth questions.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “concord” (symphonesis), symphony; “Belial” (Belial), Hebrew for worthlessness, applied to Satan. Ellicott: the only occurrence of the name in the New Testament; the Hebrew signifies “vileness, worthlessness.” Barnes: “what unison… what strings being struck will produce a chord?”

The verse adds two more questions. What harmony has Christ with Belial? What share has a believer with an unbeliever? Christ and Satan, faith and unbelief, can strike no common chord.

So the witnesses see Paul intensify the appeal with the starkest of the contrasts. The witnesses note the musical figure in “concord” — a symphony, a chord struck in unison; there is no note that Christ and Belial can sound together. They mark the name “Belial” — a Hebrew word for worthlessness and vileness, here applied to Satan, the prince of the ungodly world — set in absolute opposition to Christ. The question is rhetorical: between Christ and Satan there is no harmony, no agreement whatever. They observe the fourth question drawing the application to persons: “what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?” — the believer, united to Christ, can have no common portion or shared lot with the unbeliever, who belongs to the opposite kingdom. They note that the contrasts mount in intensity — from righteousness and light to the very persons of Christ and Belial, faith and unbelief. The verse thus drives home the incompatibility at its sharpest: as Christ and Satan are utterly opposed, so the believer and the unbeliever have no true partnership. It presses the call to separation by appeal to the deepest of all divisions — that between the kingdom of Christ and the kingdom of darkness.

So the witnesses see Paul intensify the appeal with the starkest of the contrasts. The musical figure in “concord” pictures a chord struck in unison; there is no note Christ and Belial can sound together. “Belial” — a Hebrew word for worthlessness, here applied to Satan — is set in absolute opposition to Christ. The fourth question draws the application to persons: “what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?” — the believer, united to Christ, can have no common portion with the unbeliever of the opposite kingdom. The contrasts mount in intensity, driving home the incompatibility at its sharpest — the division between the kingdom of Christ and the kingdom of darkness.
16:
On 2 Corinthians 6:16 — “And what agreement hath the temple of God with idols? for ye are the temple of the living God; as God hath said, I will dwell in them, and walk in them; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people” — the witnesses mark the temple of God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the climax. Robertson: “agreement” (synkatathesis), the fifth such word; “we are the temple” (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:16). Ellicott traces it to the controversy over things sacrificed to idols. Calvin: idolatry had ceased to be reckoned a sin among them. Wesley: “if God would not endure idols in any part of the land… how much less, under his own roof!”

The verse reaches the climax: the church is God’s temple. What agreement has God’s temple with idols? You are the temple of the living God, who dwells and walks in you, and is your God. The indwelling of God forbids alliance with idols.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the call to separation in the church’s identity as God’s temple. The witnesses note the last and decisive question: “what agreement hath the temple of God with idols?” — between the dwelling of the true God and the images of false gods there can be no concord, as the ark and Dagon could not stand together. They mark the great reason given: “ye are the temple of the living God” — the believers, collectively, are the sanctuary in which God himself dwells by his Spirit. They observe the rich promise Paul weaves from Scripture: “I will dwell in them, and walk in them; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people” — the covenant promise of God’s indwelling presence and mutual belonging. They note Wesley’s force: if God would not tolerate idols anywhere in his land, how much less under his own roof, in the temple of his people. The verse thus supplies the deepest ground for separation: because God dwells in his people as in a temple, they cannot harbour the idols and unbelief of the world. The dignity of being God’s dwelling demands holiness — and grounds the summons to come out and be separate that follows.
17:
On 2 Corinthians 6:17 — “Wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing; and I will receive you” — the witnesses mark the summons to separation and the promise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: “saith the Lord” (Isaiah 52:11; Ezekiel 20:34); “unclean thing” (akathartou). Barnes: from Isaiah 52:11, of the return from Babylon. Ellicott: the priests and Levites not to bring back the unclean. Wesley: “touch not the unclean person — keep at the utmost distance… and I will receive you.”

The verse issues the command and the gracious promise. Therefore come out from among them, be separate, says the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing; and I will receive you. Separation is met by God’s welcome.

So the witnesses see Paul gather the appeal into a direct summons, sealed by God’s own word and promise. The witnesses note the threefold command, drawn from Isaiah’s call to the exiles leaving Babylon: “come out from among them” — withdraw from the fellowship of idolaters and unbelievers; “be ye separate” — stand apart as a distinct, holy people; and “touch not the unclean thing” — keep at the utmost distance from all that defiles. They mark that, as the returning priests were not to carry back the unclean things of Babylon, so God’s people are to leave behind the pollutions of the world. They observe the gracious promise attached: “and I will receive you” — God welcomes into his presence and family those who separate themselves for him. They note that the separation is not bare negation but the condition of fuller communion: those who come out from defilement are taken in by God. The verse thus turns the doctrine of the temple into a practical command with a promise: forsake the unclean alliances of unbelief, and God himself will receive you — a welcome amplified into adoption in the closing verse.

So the witnesses see Paul gather the appeal into a direct summons, sealed by God’s own word and promise. The threefold command, drawn from Isaiah’s call to the exiles leaving Babylon: “come out from among them” — withdraw from the fellowship of idolaters; “be ye separate” — stand apart as a holy people; and “touch not the unclean thing” — keep at the utmost distance from all that defiles. As the returning priests were not to carry back the unclean things of Babylon, so God’s people leave behind the world’s pollutions. The gracious promise is attached: “and I will receive you” — separation the condition of fuller communion.
18:
On 2 Corinthians 6:18 — “And will be a Father unto you, and ye shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty” — the witnesses mark the promise of adoption.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the composite citation. Robertson: “saith the Lord Almighty” (pantokrator, Ruler of all), from 2 Samuel 7:8. Ellicott: a mosaic — “I will be a Father” (2 Samuel 7:14), “sons and daughters” (Isaiah 43:6). Wesley: the promise to Solomon “applied to all believers… who can conceive the dignity of this divine adoption?”

The verse crowns the promise with adoption. I will be a Father to you, and you shall be my sons and daughters, says the Lord Almighty. God receives the separated as his own children.

So the witnesses see Paul crown the call to separation with the highest of promises — the adoption of God’s people as his children. The witnesses note that the promise woven from Scripture rises from God’s receiving (verse 17) to his fathering: “I will be a Father unto you, and ye shall be my sons and daughters.” They mark the tenderness and dignity of the relation: God himself, the “Lord Almighty,” the Ruler of all, condescends to be Father to those who come out from the world, and exalts them to be his sons and daughters — a promise once made to David’s house, now applied to all believers. They observe the contrast that crowns the argument: those who forsake the unequal yoke with unbelief lose nothing, for they gain the infinite dignity of being children of the Almighty. They note Wesley’s wonder — who can conceive the worth of this divine adoption? The verse thus seals the appeal to holiness with the sweetest of motives: separation from the world is rewarded by the embrace of God as Father. The temple of the living God is also the family of God; and the people who keep themselves unspotted are owned as his own dear children — a promise that grounds the call to cleansing in the verse that follows in the next chapter.
